Russian pair skater (born 1993)

Antonio Souza-Kordeiru

|  |  |

**Antonio Laudelino Souza-Kordeiru** ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Антонио Лауделино Соуза-Кордейру, born 21 March 1993) is a retired Russian [pair skater](/wiki/Pair_skater "Pair skater"). Skating with [Lana Petranović](/wiki/Lana_Petranovi%C4%87 "Lana Petranović") for [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), he has competed in the final segment at four [European Championships](/wiki/European_Figure_Skating_Championships "European Figure Skating Championships").

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Souza-Kordeiru was born on 21 March 1993 in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), Russia.[[2]](#cite_note-FSR-AS-2) His mother is Russian and his father is from [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), of Afro-Cuban heritage.[[3]](#cite_note-CSF161107-3)

## Skating career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Skating career")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

Souza-Kordeiru began learning to skate in 2000.[[4]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-4) He skated in partnership with Evgenia Kazantseva before teaming up with Arina Cherniavskaia.[[5]](#cite_note-fskate-5) Representing Russia, Cherniavskaia/Souza-Kordeiru made their international debut in November 2012, taking the junior bronze medal at the [Warsaw Cup](/wiki/Warsaw_Cup "Warsaw Cup").

### 2013–2014 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2013–2014 season")]

In August 2013, Cherniavskaia/Souza-Kordeiru appeared at their first [ISU Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "ISU Junior Grand Prix") (JGP) event. Coached by [Arina Ushakova](/wiki/Arina_Ushakova_(pair_skater) "Arina Ushakova (pair skater)") and Andrei Hekalo in Moscow, they placed 5th at the [2013 JGP](/wiki/2013%E2%80%932014_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2013–2014 ISU Junior Grand Prix") in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga"), Latvia, and then won silver at their next JGP assignment, which took place in October in [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"), Czech Republic.[[1]](#cite_note-ISU-1314-1)

### 2014–2015 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2014–2015 season")]

Cherniavskaia/Souza-Kordeiru moved up to the senior level, coached by Ushakova.[[6]](#cite_note-ISU-1415-6) Making their [Grand Prix](/wiki/ISU_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating "ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating") debut, the pair placed eighth at the [2014 Cup of China](/wiki/2014_Cup_of_China "2014 Cup of China") in November. Later that month, they competed at the [2014 CS Ice Challenge](/wiki/2014_CS_Ice_Challenge "2014 CS Ice Challenge") and [2014 CS Warsaw Cup](/wiki/2014_CS_Warsaw_Cup "2014 CS Warsaw Cup"), finishing fourth at both events, but withdrew from their second Grand Prix assignment, the [2014 NHK Trophy](/wiki/2014_NHK_Trophy "2014 NHK Trophy"). They placed eighth at the [2015 Russian Championships](/wiki/2015_Russian_Figure_Skating_Championships "2015 Russian Figure Skating Championships").

### 2015–2016 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2015–2016 season")]

In October, Cherniavskaia/Souza-Kordeiru finished fourth at the [2015 CS Mordovian Ornament](/wiki/2015_CS_Mordovian_Ornament "2015 CS Mordovian Ornament"). It was their final international together. The pair did not compete at the [2016 Russian Championships](/wiki/2016_Russian_Figure_Skating_Championships "2016 Russian Figure Skating Championships").

In 2016, Croatia's [Lana Petranović](/wiki/Lana_Petranovi%C4%87 "Lana Petranović") arrived in Moscow in search of a skating partner.[[7]](#cite_note-RBTH170214-7) She and Souza-Kordeiru teamed up in spring 2016 to compete for Croatia.[[4]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-4)

### 2016–2017 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2016–2017 season")]

Making their international debut as a team, Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed fourth at the [2016 CS Warsaw Cup](/wiki/2016_CS_Warsaw_Cup "2016 CS Warsaw Cup") in November. They finished fifth at the [2016 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2016_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2016 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb") in December; 15th at the [2017 European Championships](/wiki/2017_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 European Figure Skating Championships") in January in [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"), Czech Republic; and 21st at the [2017 World Championships](/wiki/2017_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2017 World Figure Skating Championships") in March in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland. They were coached by [Yuri Larionov](/wiki/Yuri_Larionov "Yuri Larionov") in Moscow.[[3]](#cite_note-CSF161107-3)[[8]](#cite_note-ISU-1617-8)

### 2017–2018 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2017–2018 season")]

Petranović underwent an operation after injuring the [meniscus](/wiki/Meniscus_(anatomy) "Meniscus (anatomy)") in her knee and returned to the ice after three months.[[9]](#cite_note-RBTH171214-9) Due to her injury, the pair was unable to compete in September at the [2017 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2017_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2017 CS Nebelhorn Trophy"), which served as the final [qualification opportunity for the 2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Qualification"). They returned to competition in January, placing 12th at the [2018 European Championships](/wiki/2018_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2018 European Figure Skating Championships") in Moscow. In March, they placed 21st at the [2018 World Championships](/wiki/2018_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2018 World Figure Skating Championships") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy. They were coached by Larionov and Dmitri Savin in Moscow.[[10]](#cite_note-ISU-1718-10)

### 2018–2019 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2018–2019 season")]

Coached by Savin in Moscow,[[4]](#cite_note-ISU-1819-4) Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed seventh at their first event of the season, the [2018 CS Lombardia Trophy](/wiki/2018_CS_Lombardia_Trophy "2018 CS Lombardia Trophy"). They competed at two other [Challenger events](/wiki/2018-19_ISU_Challenger_Series "2018-19 ISU Challenger Series") of the season, the [2018 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2018_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2018 CS Nebelhorn Trophy") and [2018 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2018_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2018 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb"), placing eighth and seventh. They finished the season placing eighth at the [2019 European Championships](/wiki/2019_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 European Figure Skating Championships") and sixteenth at the [2019 World Championships](/wiki/2019_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2019 World Figure Skating Championships").[[11]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-11)

### 2019–2020 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2019–2020 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed twelfth at the [2019 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2019_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2019 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb") and fifteenth at the [2020 European Championships](/wiki/2020_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 European Figure Skating Championships"). The [2020 World Championships](/wiki/2020_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2020 World Figure Skating Championships") were cancelled as a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[11]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-11)

### 2020–2021 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020–2021 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru placed twenty-first at the [2021 World Championships](/wiki/2021_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "2021 World Figure Skating Championships").[[11]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-11)

### 2021–2022 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2021–2022 season")]

Petranović/Souza-Kordeiru began the season at the [2021 CS Nebelhorn Trophy](/wiki/2021_CS_Nebelhorn_Trophy "2021 CS Nebelhorn Trophy"), seeking to [qualify](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Qualification "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Qualification") a berth at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics"). They placed twelfth, outside of qualification. They went on to finish sixth at the [Budapest Trophy](/wiki/Budapest_Trophy "Budapest Trophy") and eleventh at the [2021 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb](/wiki/2021_CS_Golden_Spin_of_Zagreb "2021 CS Golden Spin of Zagreb"), and then sixteenth at the [2022 European Championships](/wiki/2022_European_Figure_Skating_Championships "2022 European Figure Skating Championships").[[11]](#cite_note-ISU-LPAS-11)

In February, he announced his retirement from competitive figure skating.[[12]](#cite_note-sk-ret-12)

## Programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Souza-Kordeiru&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Programs")]

### With Petranović
